lookup_context: Change number: 3
Question is asking for the relationship between coordination cutoff and publication effective date
items.task: Use the row for Change 3 and identify both dates in the correct roles.
items.question_text: For 7110.65BB Change 3, which cutoff/effective-date pair follows from TBL 1-1-1?

choices.text: Cutoff January 22, 2026; effective publication July 9, 2026.
Cutoff July 9, 2026; effective publication January 22, 2026.
Cutoff and effective publication both July 9, 2026.
The table gives only change numbers; it does not assign dates.

items.explanation: TBL 1-1-1 separates the date coordination must be completed from the later publication effective date. For Change 3, those are January 22, 2026 and July 9, 2026.

lookup_context: The controller is entering aircraft identity on a flight progress strip
The figure shows the standard hand-printed characters
items.task: Decide whether local stylized handwriting is an acceptable substitute for the depicted standard characters.
items.question_text: A controller uses locally stylized handwriting for strip entries because the aircraft identity is obvious from context. How should that be evaluated against FIG 2-3-1?

choices.text: Not acceptable; the figure supports standard hand-printed characters to avoid misinterpretation.
Acceptable whenever the controller can infer the aircraft identity from context.
Acceptable only for numerals, but not for letters.
Acceptable if the strip is used locally and will not be handed off.

items.explanation: FIG 2-3-1 is not testing artistic style; it supports standardization so strip entries are not misread. Context is not a substitute for standard strip marking.

lookup_context: Aircraft is being cleared to taxi to a runway
The route may appear obvious on the airport diagram
Runway crossings and hold-short points are operationally significant
items.task: Decide what the taxi clearance must communicate, not merely whether a diagram exists.
items.instruction: Use the taxi-route examples as applied examples.
items.question_text: A taxi clearance to a runway omits the specific taxi route because the airport diagram makes the route seem obvious. Which decision follows from the 3-7-2 examples?

choices.text: The clearance is deficient; the taxi route must be stated, including runway crossing or hold-short instructions when applicable.
The clearance is acceptable because the diagram supplies the missing route.
The clearance is acceptable if no other aircraft are on the movement area.
The route may be omitted only when the aircraft is taxiing to a departure runway.

items.explanation: The examples reinforce the operational rule: a runway taxi clearance must communicate the specific route and any runway crossing or hold-short requirement. The diagram does not replace the clearance.

lookup_context: Succeeding departure is behind another aircraft on the same runway
Preceding aircraft is airborne
The prompt does not state runway-end crossing, a turn to avert conflict, or a required landmark distance
items.task: Use the figure logic to decide whether airborne status alone satisfies same-runway departure separation.
items.question_text: A succeeding departure begins takeoff roll solely because the preceding same-runway aircraft is airborne. What does the figure logic require you to conclude?

choices.text: Do not treat airborne status alone as enough; one of the depicted separation conditions must be satisfied.
Airborne status alone is enough for all same-runway departure cases.
Airborne status is enough only if both aircraft are Category I.
The succeeding aircraft may roll if the preceding aircraft is past midfield.

items.explanation: The figures distinguish specific acceptable conditions, such as crossing the runway end, turning to avert conflict, or satisfying the landmark-distance alternative. Merely being airborne is not the operative test.

lookup_context: Time: between sunrise and sunset
Suitable landmarks permit distance determination
Follower: Category II arrival
Preceding aircraft: landed Category I aircraft
Measured distance from threshold: at least 4,500 feet
items.task: Decide whether the threshold-crossing condition is satisfied before the preceding aircraft is clear.
items.question_text: A Category II arrival follows a landed Category I aircraft. In daylight, landmarks show at least 4,500 feet from the landing threshold. May the arrival cross the threshold before the other aircraft is clear?

choices.text: Yes, if the daylight and landmark conditions are met, 4,500 feet satisfies the Category II behind Category I/II alternative.
No, the preceding aircraft must always be clear before the following aircraft crosses the threshold.
No, the 4,500-foot alternative applies only to Category I followers.
Yes, but only if radar separation is also being applied.

items.explanation: For a Category II arrival behind a landed Category I or II aircraft, the daylight landmark-distance alternative is 4,500 feet from the threshold.

lookup_context: ATC is specifying a route other than an established airway or route
A portion of the route lies within controlled airspace
The route uses NAVAID-based altitude and distance limits
items.task: Decide whether each controlled-airspace portion must remain within the applicable altitude/radius limits.
items.question_text: When building a non-airway route, may ATC rely on the route merely touching a NAVAID service volume somewhere along the route?

choices.text: No. Each controlled-airspace portion of the specified route must remain within the applicable usable altitude and distance limits.
Yes. Any contact with a NAVAID service volume validates the entire route segment.
Yes, if the aircraft is already radar identified.
No, unless the route is below 14,500 feet.

items.explanation: FIG 4-1-1 and FIG 4-1-2 illustrate application of the table limits. The limit applies to the route portion in controlled airspace, not just to an isolated point along the route.

lookup_context: Aircraft is not yet at an IAF, IF, feeder route, or published segment
Aircraft is only generally pointed toward the airport
The question is about when an approach clearance may be issued
items.task: Decide whether general alignment is enough, or whether the figure-supported entry conditions must be met.
items.question_text: A pilot is generally pointed toward the airport but has not reached an IAF, IF, feeder route, or published segment and does not meet a stated direct-to/intercept condition. May ATC clear the aircraft for the approach?

choices.text: No. The aircraft must enter through one of the figure-supported published-segment, fix, feeder, direct-to, or intercept conditions.
Yes. General alignment toward the airport is enough.
Yes, if the pilot has the airport in sight.
No, unless the aircraft is on a STAR.

items.explanation: The approach-clearance figures support the entry rules. A general heading toward the airport is not a substitute for being established on, or cleared to properly intercept, the procedure structure.

lookup_context: Operation: terminal aircraft on approach to the same runway
Leader wake category: C
Follower wake category: H
Apply the value when the preceding aircraft is over the landing threshold
items.task: Find the intersection of leader C and follower H in the on-approach wake table.
items.instruction: Use the terminal on-approach wake turbulence table.
items.question_text: For a Category H follower behind a Category C leader on approach to the same runway, what spacing does TBL 5-5-2 require?

choices.text: 5 NM, existing when the preceding aircraft is over the landing threshold.
3 NM, because normal terminal radar separation controls all approach wake cases.
8 NM, because Category H followers always use the maximum table value.
No wake minimum applies once both aircraft are established on final.

items.explanation: TBL 5-5-2 gives 5 NM for a Category H follower behind a Category C leader on approach, applied when the preceding aircraft is over the landing threshold.

lookup_context: Aircraft type: non-helicopter
Intercept point: 3 miles from the approach gate
Question is asking for the maximum final-approach-course intercept angle
items.task: Use the row for interception 2 miles or more from the approach gate and avoid applying the helicopter exception.
items.question_text: A non-helicopter aircraft is vectored to intercept final 3 miles from the approach gate. What maximum intercept angle applies under TBL 5-9-1?

choices.text: 30 degrees.
20 degrees.
45 degrees.
60 degrees.

items.explanation: TBL 5-9-1 allows 30 degrees for non-helicopter interception 2 miles or more from the approach gate. The 45-degree value is a helicopter-specific allowance.

lookup_context: Runways are adjacent and 3,000 feet apart
Aircraft are successive on adjacent final approach courses
Question is about the diagonal spacing minimum after establishment on final
items.task: Choose the spacing rule that applies after both aircraft are established on adjacent final approach courses.
items.instruction: Apply the simultaneous dependent approach minima.
items.question_text: For simultaneous dependent approaches to adjacent runways 3,000 feet apart, what spacing rule applies between successive aircraft after they are established on adjacent final approach courses?

choices.text: Apply the 1 NM diagonal minimum between successive aircraft after they are established on adjacent final approach courses.
Apply only 1,000 feet vertical separation after both aircraft are established on final.
Use 3 miles radar separation until both aircraft land.
No diagonal minimum applies once both aircraft are on adjacent finals.

items.explanation: The dependent-approach rule uses the 1 NM diagonal minimum after aircraft are established on adjacent final approach courses. The turn-on phase has separate vertical or radar separation requirements.

lookup_context: Separation method: non-DME
Same NAVAID radials
Divergence: 32 degrees
The divergence value falls between two listed table rows
items.task: Use the lesser listed divergence value rather than interpolating upward.
items.question_text: Two aircraft use non-DME separation on same-NAVAID radials diverging 32 degrees. Which table value is used?

choices.text: Use the 30-degree value, requiring 8 NM.
Use the 35-degree value, requiring 7 NM.
Interpolate between 30 and 35 degrees.
Use the 45-degree value because 32 degrees is closer to 45 than to 15.

items.explanation: When the divergence falls between listed values, use the lesser divergence value. For the non-DME table, 30 degrees requires 8 NM.

lookup_context: Same-NAVAID VOR/VORTAC/TACAN tracks
Divergence band: 26 to 35 degrees
Altitude: FL230 and below
items.task: Locate the divergence band and altitude band, then choose the flight distance before protected airspace is clear.
items.question_text: For same-NAVAID VOR/VORTAC/TACAN tracks diverging 26 to 35 degrees at FL230 and below, what flight distance is required before protected airspace is clear?

choices.text: 11 miles.
13 miles.
17 miles.
30 miles.

items.explanation: TBL 8-4-1 gives 11 miles for same-NAVAID VOR/VORTAC/TACAN tracks diverging 26 to 35 degrees at FL230 and below.

lookup_context: Operation: domestic CPDLC
The tables define an approved message set
Immediate or expeditious clearances still require judgment about communication method
items.task: Decide how the table-defined message set interacts with voice requirements.
items.instruction: Use the domestic CPDLC message-table rule.
items.question_text: How should the domestic CPDLC message tables be used operationally when a clearance is immediate or expeditious?

choices.text: Treat the tables as the approved message set that augments voice; immediate or expeditious clearances normally remain on voice unless voice is not operationally feasible.
Move all urgent clearances to CPDLC whenever a matching message element exists.
Treat the message tables as advisory examples only, with no operational effect.
Use CPDLC tables as a full replacement for normal voice communication in domestic operations.

items.explanation: The domestic CPDLC tables define the available message set, but the paragraph still limits CPDLC use for critical phases and immediate or expeditious clearances.

lookup_context: Operation: oceanic CPDLC
The table-defined message set is available
Free text may create closure and interpretation issues
items.task: Choose the operating principle for using table-defined CPDLC messages versus free text.
items.instruction: Use the oceanic CPDLC message-table rule.
items.question_text: What operating principle governs use of the oceanic CPDLC message tables?

choices.text: Use the CPDLC message set instead of free text to the extent possible because it supports proper closure of exchanges.
Prefer free text because it avoids the constraints of the message set.
Use CPDLC only when VHF coverage exists, then switch to free text outside VHF coverage.
Use the message tables only for post-event review, not live controller-pilot communications.

items.explanation: For oceanic CPDLC, the table-defined message set should be used instead of free text to the extent possible because it improves standardization and closure of exchanges.
